title: Exercises: Computational Complexity
description: Analyze algorithms and explore complexity classes

exercises.title: Big-O Analysis
exercises.description: Analyze the time complexity of various algorithms empirically.
hints: When n doubles, O(n) time doubles, O(n²) time quadruples
O(n log n) grows slightly faster than O(n)

exercises.solution:
import math

def classical_search_complexity(n: int) -> int:
    """Classical search: O(N)"""
    return n

def quantum_search_complexity(n: int) -> int:
    """Grover's search: O(√N)"""
    return int(math.sqrt(n))

def classical_factoring_complexity(bits: int) -> float:
    """
    Best classical factoring (number field sieve).
    O(exp(c * n^(1/3) * (log n)^(2/3)))
    Simplified: roughly O(exp(1.9 * n^(1/3) * (ln n)^(2/3)))
    """
    n = bits
    c = 1.9  # Constant for NFS
    exponent = c * (n ** (1/3)) * (math.log(n) ** (2/3))
    return math.exp(exponent)

def quantum_factoring_complexity(bits: int) -> int:
    """Shor's algorithm: O(n³)"""
    return bits ** 3

def search_speedup(database_size: int) -> float:
    """Calculate Grover speedup factor."""
    classical = classical_search_complexity(database_size)
    quantum = quantum_search_complexity(database_size)
    return classical / quantum if quantum > 0 else float('inf')

def factoring_speedup(bits: int) -> float:
    """Calculate Shor speedup factor."""
    classical = classical_factoring_complexity(bits)
    quantum = quantum_factoring_complexity(bits)
    return classical / quantum if quantum > 0 else float('inf')

def compare_speedups():
    """Compare classical vs quantum for various problem sizes."""
    print("=== Grover's Search Speedup ===")
    print("Database Size | Classical | Quantum | Speedup")
    print("-" * 50)
    for n in [100, 1000, 10000, 1000000, 10**9]:
        c = classical_search_complexity(n)
        q = quantum_search_complexity(n)
        speedup = c / q
        print(f"{n:>13} | {c:>9} | {q:>7} | {speedup:.1f}x")
    
    print("\n=== Shor's Factoring Speedup ===")
    print("Bits | Classical (ops) | Quantum (ops) | Speedup")
    print("-" * 60)
    for bits in [64, 128, 256, 512, 1024, 2048]:
        c = classical_factoring_complexity(bits)
        q = quantum_factoring_complexity(bits)
        speedup = c / q
        print(f"{bits:>4} | {c:>15.2e} | {q:>13} | {speedup:.2e}")
    
    print("\n=== Key Insights ===")
    print("1. Grover: Quadratic speedup (√N vs N)")
    print("   - 1 billion items: 31623x speedup")
    print("")
    print("2. Shor: Exponential speedup")
    print("   - 2048-bit RSA: ~10^50 speedup!")
    print("")
    print("3. Practical implications:")
    print("   - Grover: Doubles effective key length of symmetric crypto")
    print("   - Shor: Breaks RSA, ECC completely")

compare_speedups()

# Additional analysis
print("\n=== Time Estimates ===")
print("Assuming 1 billion operations/second:")
for bits in [512, 1024, 2048]:
    c_ops = classical_factoring_complexity(bits)
    q_ops = quantum_factoring_complexity(bits)
    
    c_seconds = c_ops / 1e9
    q_seconds = q_ops / 1e9
    
    # Convert to readable time
    def format_time(seconds):
        if seconds < 60:
            return f"{seconds:.2f} seconds"
        elif seconds < 3600:
            return f"{seconds/60:.2f} minutes"
        elif seconds < 86400:
            return f"{seconds/3600:.2f} hours"
        elif seconds < 365.25*86400:
            return f"{seconds/86400:.2f} days"
        else:
            years = seconds / (365.25*86400)
            if years > 1e10:
                return f"{years:.2e} years"
            return f"{years:.2f} years"
    
    print(f"\n{bits}-bit factoring:")
    print(f"  Classical: {format_time(c_seconds)}")
    print(f"  Quantum:   {format_time(q_seconds)}")
hints: Grover: O(√N) vs O(N) = quadratic speedup
Shor: O(n³) vs O(exp(n^⅓)) = exponential speedup

exercises.title: P vs NP Exploration
exercises.description: Implement brute-force solvers and verifiers to understand the finding vs verifying distinction.
exercises.starterCode:
import time
import itertools

def solve_subset_sum_bruteforce(numbers: list, target: int):
    """
    Find a subset that sums to target using brute force.
    Complexity: O(2^n)
    """
    # Your code here
    pass

def verify_subset_sum(numbers: list, target: int, subset: list) -> bool:
    """Verify solution in O(n)."""
    return set(subset).issubset(set(numbers)) and sum(subset) == target

def compare_solve_vs_verify(n_values: list):
    """
    Compare time to solve vs time to verify for different input sizes.
    Demonstrates the P vs NP gap.
    """
    # Your code here
    pass

# Run comparison
compare_solve_vs_verify([10, 15, 20])
exercises.solution:
import time
import itertools
import random

def solve_subset_sum_bruteforce(numbers: list, target: int):
    """Find a subset summing to target - O(2^n)."""
    n = len(numbers)
    for r in range(n + 1):
        for subset in itertools.combinations(numbers, r):
            if sum(subset) == target:
                return list(subset)
    return None

def verify_subset_sum(numbers: list, target: int, subset: list) -> bool:
    """Verify solution in O(n)."""
    if not subset:
        return target == 0
    for num in subset:
        if num not in numbers:
            return False
    return sum(subset) == target

def solve_3sat_bruteforce(num_vars: int, clauses: list):
    """Solve 3-SAT by trying all assignments - O(2^n)."""
    for bits in range(2 ** num_vars):
        assignment = {i+1: bool((bits >> i) & 1) for i in range(num_vars)}
        
        satisfied = True
        for clause in clauses:
            clause_sat = False
            for lit in clause:
                var = abs(lit)
                val = assignment[var]
                if (lit > 0 and val) or (lit < 0 and not val):
                    clause_sat = True
                    break
            if not clause_sat:
                satisfied = False
                break
        
        if satisfied:
            return assignment
    return None

def verify_3sat(clauses: list, assignment: dict) -> bool:
    """Verify 3-SAT solution - O(clauses × 3)."""
    for clause in clauses:
        satisfied = False
        for lit in clause:
            var = abs(lit)
            val = assignment.get(var, False)
            if (lit > 0 and val) or (lit < 0 and not val):
                satisfied = True
                break
        if not satisfied:
            return False
    return True

def compare_solve_vs_verify(n_values: list):
    """Compare solving vs verifying times."""
    print("=== Subset Sum: Solve vs Verify ===")
    print("n | Solve Time | Verify Time | Ratio")
    print("-" * 45)
    
    for n in n_values:
        # Create random instance
        numbers = [random.randint(1, 100) for _ in range(n)]
        # Pick target that has solution
        subset_size = n // 2
        solution = random.sample(numbers, subset_size)
        target = sum(solution)
        
        # Time solving
        start = time.time()
        found = solve_subset_sum_bruteforce(numbers, target)
        solve_time = time.time() - start
        
        # Time verification
        start = time.time()
        for _ in range(1000):  # Repeat to get measurable time
            valid = verify_subset_sum(numbers, target, found)
        verify_time = (time.time() - start) / 1000
        
        ratio = solve_time / verify_time if verify_time > 0 else float('inf')
        print(f"{n:>2} | {solve_time:>10.4f}s | {verify_time:>11.6f}s | {ratio:>8.0f}x")
    
    print("\n=== 3-SAT: Solve vs Verify ===")
    print("vars | clauses | Solve Time | Verify Time | Ratio")
    print("-" * 55)
    
    for num_vars in [8, 10, 12, 14]:
        # Generate random 3-SAT instance
        num_clauses = num_vars * 4  # Standard ratio
        clauses = []
        for _ in range(num_clauses):
            clause = random.sample(range(1, num_vars + 1), 3)
            clause = [v if random.random() > 0.5 else -v for v in clause]
            clauses.append(clause)
        
        # Time solving
        start = time.time()
        solution = solve_3sat_bruteforce(num_vars, clauses)
        solve_time = time.time() - start
        
        if solution:
            # Time verification
            start = time.time()
            for _ in range(10000):
                valid = verify_3sat(clauses, solution)
            verify_time = (time.time() - start) / 10000
            
            ratio = solve_time / verify_time if verify_time > 0 else float('inf')
            print(f"{num_vars:>4} | {num_clauses:>7} | {solve_time:>10.4f}s | {verify_time:>11.7f}s | {ratio:>8.0f}x")
        else:
            print(f"{num_vars:>4} | {num_clauses:>7} | {solve_time:>10.4f}s | No solution found")
    
    print("\n=== Key Observation ===")
    print("As n grows, solving time grows EXPONENTIALLY (2^n)")
    print("but verification time grows only LINEARLY (n)")
    print("This is the essence of the P vs NP question!")

compare_solve_vs_verify([10, 15, 18, 20])
hints: Solving is O(2^n), verification is O(n)
The ratio shows the P vs NP gap
